editableNewDialogue.text: I keep seeing headlines that recycle last year’s “top tech stories.” How would you tell a real breakthrough from a recap?

A recap usually summarizes known events (“best of,” “top,” “biggest trends”). A breakthrough claim tends to include: (1) a specific new result (metric, benchmark, prototype), (2) who achieved it (lab/company), (3) where it was disclosed (paper, conference, regulator filing), and (4) what changed versus prior art. If none of those are present, it’s often just commentary.
What are quick red flags that it’s hype?

Red flags: vague superlatives (“revolutionary,” “game-changing”) without numbers; no named researchers or institutions; no link to a primary source; “could”/“might” everywhere; and comparisons to unrelated tech (e.g., calling everything “the new internet”). Also watch for recycled images and identical phrasing across many sites.
Okay. Give me a simple checklist I can apply in under a minute.

60-second checklist:
1) Is there a primary source link (paper, preprint, patent, dataset, standards draft)?
2) Is the claim measurable (speedup %, error rate, energy, cost, scale)?
3) Is the baseline stated (what it beats, and how)?
4) Any independent confirmation (another lab, replication, benchmark suite)?
5) Is the limitation mentioned (constraints, failures, scope)?
If you get 3+ “no” answers, treat it as a recap or marketing.
Tangential question: is it true the first website ever is still online?
Yes—CERN maintains the first website at info.cern.ch, which documents the original World Wide Web project.

That list you just pulled up is all "best assistants" stuff again—can we not do this loop? If it can’t be pinned to Jan 2, 2024 with an actual breakthrough, toss it; I’m trying to post the *new* stuff, not evergreen productivity roundups.
Understood—I'll filter out evergreen/tool roundup results and keep only items that clearly indicate a new tech breakthrough and tie back to Jan 2, 2024 before drafting anything for Instagram.

No—it's not that I hate 2023 tech news. I just don't want those 'Top tech stories of 2023' recap lists. If there's an actual breakthrough that happened in 2023, that's fine—just make sure it's a real new breakthrough, not a yearly roundup.

Got it. You’re not rejecting 2023 because of the year—you’re rejecting recap-style 'top stories of 2023' summaries. You still want genuine breakthrough items (even if they’re dated 2023), as long as they’re actual new developments rather than roundups.
